text: A seventeen-year-old male presented to his primary care provider with cough and chest pain. A chest x-ray showed a nodular opacity, so a CT scan was obtained for further characterization. The CT demonstrated an anterior mediastinal mass measuring five point zero by six point eight by ten point eight centimeters. The lesion contained macroscopic fat and abuts the brachiocephalic vein. Tumor markers including AFP, beta HGG, and LDH were negative.
Informed consent was obtained and the patient was brought to the operating room. General anesthetic was administered and the patient was intubated with a dual lumen endotracheal tube.

The patient is positioned supine with a bump elevating the right side of the body. A 5mm incision is made in the right anterior chest at the sixth intercostal space. Under direct visualization, two additional 5mm ports are placed in the mid axillary line.

Upon entry into the mediastinum, the surgeon observes the mass bulging into the right chest. The mammary vessels are positioned anterior to the mass, while the phrenic nerve is located posteriorly. Key structures such as the azygos vein, superior intercostal vein, intercostal veins, superior vena cava, phrenic nerve, and mammary vessels are identified. The medial aspect of the right lung is draped over the mass, and a dissection plane is created between the visceral pleura and the pleura overlying the mass.

The surgeon uses hook cautery for anterior dissection between the mediastinal mass and the sternum, extending the dissection into the left pleural space. A fourth port is placed in the midaxillary line to aid in dissection. The mass is carefully dissected off the innominate vein using a combination of ligature and blunt dissection with the endokitner, requiring significant traction to mobilize it from the innominate vein and aorta. The surgeon then focuses on the portion of the mass adherent to the pericardium, using the endokitner for careful dissection.

The surgeon retracts the mass posteriorly after freeing it cranially and caudally, mobilizing the left lateral aspect off the pleura of the left lung. An additional trocar is placed in the left chest at the fifth intercostal space in the midaxillary line to visualize the left phrenic nerve, ensuring it remains intact. The mass is then mobilized into the right chest and out of the mediastinum.

The mediastinum is hemostatic, and the intact pericardium is visible. The surgeon enlarges the middle port site in the right midaxillary line to exteriorize the mass using a fifteen millimeter endocutch bag. The right chest is irrigated, and a nineteen French Blake drain is placed through the inferior site into the mediastinal space.

A postoperative chest x-ray was obtained, which demonstrated the thoracostomy tube in place and no hemothorax or pneumothorax. The postoperative course was uncomplicated. The thoracostomy tube was removed on postoperative day three, and the patient was discharged that same day.

Ultimately, pathology demonstrated a multicystic teratoma consisting of skin with adenexal structures, respiratory epithelium, cartilage, small intestine epithelium, and pancreatic tissue with exocrine glands and islet cell hyperplasia. consistent with a mature cystic teratoma. The mass was eleven centimeters in greatest dimension. At the time of his four-week clinic follow-up, the patient noted that his symptoms have completely resolved.
